speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Martha Lowery Long took place at her home in Kendrick on October 25th, 1976. Also present were Marvin Long and Lee Magnusen. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long
words: How do you want this started? How do you want this?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Do you want it to be my, just what I remember, or family, my sister had a lot of family anecdotes in there that I just know real well, but I just know them from listening to family tell them. Well, those are a few of those that seem interesting, I think. But really your own experience most of all, what you remember about it being like there when you were a kid. You say it was rough, and I... Well, there was no money in those early days. They came out there and settled in the dollar, you know, it was just a fortune. There was no actual cash. We just made our fun. All our fun was made. They raised what food they could and they had to buy a few things. And they brought what they had with them when they came and settled in the homestead. The fact that it was the Quincy Valley, which was a sagebrush country, you see. Then, a flat, sagebrush covered desert country. And later became this lush, irrigated country that you go to. That's why Quincy is interesting to a lot of people. They don't know what it was like in that early days when my folks... It was opened up for homesteads, that's how they happened to go there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it that your parents actually found that? Are you taping this now?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh yeah, I just turned it up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, what are you asking?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, I'm just trying to ignore that it's... Well, they had lived in North Dakota and Dad was a carpenter, I guess. And North Dakota was a meadow, you know, a prairie country, level prairie country. And my mother believed that a family was better off growing up away from relatives rather than too close. Although she was very devoted to her relatives. And so, this Quincy Valley in Big Bend Country in Central Washington was opened up for homesteading. And you could go out and select a quarter section of land and if you made certain improvements and lived on it a certain length of time, I don't know the exact length of time, then you owned it. And that's what they did. Dad went out first and chose this, it was a mile and a half, directly north of Quincy, the town of Quincy. There wasn't much town yet, but there was a town, a small town. There was a store there then. And he chose the south west quarter section. It was all square, it wasn't like this country where sections were all irregular. They were all laid out square because it was level country. And he started to build a little house. It only had one room at first, one large room. And it had board and batten, that's boards that go up and down and then the cracks are covered with another board, you know. And a tar paper roof and a stove pipe, it had no chimney, no brick, there was no brick. And when mother first came in, he was there a year building this little house, getting started. And then mother and the four older children came and these cracks were still open. And my sister told that and that other story, how scared she was that somebody would peek in with those cracks. I was scared too. Anyway, then I was the first child born on the homestead. And I was the biggest and the healthiest and I still have the best health and the others are all gone except two sisters. And I've always laid it to that virgin soil.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They broke up this for my study of nutrition, I think that might have had something to do with my health, don't you?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Maybe a con or one day out there. Now Marvin, let me tell it. You may go tell it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is the story?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, we had a doctor, my sister got this part wrong. His name was Dr. Whidbey and he was my specialist in Wenatchee later when I lived in Wenatchee. At the time he was a young country doctor. And so when the baby was, there were no hospitals or anything, and when it was time for the baby to arrive, Dad got on one of the plow horses, we didn't have riding horses, we had plow horses, you know, just work horses. Got on one of the horses and rode to town to get the doctor. And the doctor borrowed a saddle horse from Jack Gaither, it was a bit lively, and they started out at our farm. And the horse stepped in the badger hole and threw the doctor. The horse wasn't hurt but he ran back to town and the doctor's bag settled there and the doctor picked up his bag and had to walk the rest of the way. So they always said the doctor got me out of the badger hole. And on the way to school later when I was school age and I walked to school through the sagebrush, here was this big badger hole right by the trail. And I go around and I can still remember looking down at it every time I went past thinking there must be another baby in that hole. I was sure that was the one. But I never found any babies in that badger hole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he get there in time?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, he got there in time and I was a ten pound baby, I had a good start. And I kept it up all my life and I was healthy and I think it was because they were on virgin soil and that soil was producing good vegetables. I don't think the badger hole had anything to do with my health but the virgin soil might have. You know very well it could have or my mother's healthy condition of course did too, contributed. Anyway, years later I was working in Wenatchee. This is going ahead in the story but Dr. Whidbey went to Vienna and became an eye specialist. And Dr. Whidbey fitted my first glasses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Wenatchee?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: In Wenatchee. And he belonged to, I was in extension work at that time. The county agent belonged to the Rotary Club along with this doctor. I told him, my father came to visit and told him this story about Dr. Whidbey delivering my mother when I was born. Oh he couldn't wait. So one time I was at Rotary Club as a guest with 4 -H clubs you know are on the program. So his eyes were dancing. I knew something was coming but I didn't guess what it was. He said I have an important guest to introduce and he asked me to stand and then he said I'd like for Dr. Whidbey to stand. And he told that whole Rotary Club at Wenatchee that, and Dr. Whidbey was a small man and very much liked at Wenatchee, that Dr. Whidbey delivered me when he was a young practitioner at Quincy. I liked them both so I didn't care but it was very funny. Well you know in just glancing at your sister's story, I had the idea from the glimpses that she gave of your mother that it was somewhat rough on her. The living and the... Well yes it was. Of course all pioneer women, most pioneer women had it. But they were happy doing it. I mean my mother knew how to, for us to have great fun. Oh my childhood was just happy and we had great fun as a family. There were finally 7 children, there were 2 more after me.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: 2 more born there. I was born Dr. Incembert Beevry and when I was born, the neighbor wouldn't come in and just hear me.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: But there wasn't any money in our... They broke up this sagebrush and expected to raise crops like they had in North Dakota where there was more rainfall. Well the first few years there was enough rainfall so that times were not quite so hard. And they got crops, small wheat crops. And then the climate changed in that country and it became drought. And they weren't able to even raise a crop. So then my father had to work out, whatever you could get. In the hills north of Quincy there were wheat ranches that did produce and my uncle Jim lived there. And he used to go over and work and harvest and earn money that way. And then there was another way that they earned money that was sort of interesting. They had a road...

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: what did they call it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: A road tax. They taxed everybody to build the roads in those days. And if you didn't want to pay your tax, you could hire somebody else to do the work for you. And he did that kind of work. And he'd be gone all week. He'd roll up... he used to roll up a bed of real heavy quilts. It was fun on Sunday night. We'd help him roll up the bed. And then he'd load the wagon and they'd go off. Sometimes the boys had these jobs too and they'd go off and work on the road all week and then come back. They'd do their own cooking and they'd sleep under the wagon if it rained a little. It didn't rain much in that country, but if it did... And that was the way they made living.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: The way they paid the taxes too?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: The only people that survived the droughts... we moved away. And the farm went for taxes finally because of this long drought period. And it was these German people that had moved in there that were thrifty that knew how to survive. The Russian thistles had come in as one of the pests that were ever brought up. And they're the things you see in western pictures. The big tumbleweeds that go down in the ghost towns. A lot of those were Russian thistles I think. Ours were big like that. They used to cut these in the green stage and stack them and feed them to their cattle. These German people knew how to do that and they could survive, but we didn't. We moved. My mother was determined we were having education, so we moved to Poland. But I was going to tell you about some of the fun we used to have. We used to play games as a family. And we played lots of games sitting around the big dining room table. It always had white oil cloth on it. We had a big table that would seat all nine of us. Because the youngest children didn't play much. We played games like... not cards! My mother was against cards, against liquor and cards. Baseball on Sunday and she had very strict standards about those things. As a lot of people did in those days. You were either one way or you were the other. So we didn't know anything about baseball because it was always played on Sunday. And you couldn't play games on Sunday like that. We couldn't play with a ball at home on Sunday even. We couldn't cut paper dolls on Sunday. I never understood that, but there was a hook on the wall where we'd get the sitters. Paper dolls were a big thing in our life. But Sunday you couldn't take down the sitters and cut paper dolls. Because Sunday had to be special. And that impressed us. We went to church. We got ready on Saturday. No work was ever done on the farm on Sunday. My people were Presbyterians.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We never had that kind of Presbyterians here.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well that's the way it was in those early days. There wasn't any work done on Sunday. The horses were allowed to take you to church. But the horses weren't worked. They rested too. Then we did the preacher. Oh we had a big dinner. We got up and had to kill a chicken. There wasn't any refrigeration so you had to get up and kill the chicken Sunday morning. And get it started cooking. And then bring the preacher home for dinner. My mother worked hard, but that was all for the good of the Lord. Because it was the preacher she was feeding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he come to dinner?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh he came to dinner often. The preacher was like a member of our family. He was always one of my mother's best friends. And the first preacher I remember was They called him He was quite well known in that country. He had several small They called him He was like an itinerant preacher. They called him the jackrabbit preacher Because jackrabbits were prevalent in the country. And he came from one town to the other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a circuit rider?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes on that order. He used to ride a bicycle. The great northern railroad went through Quincy And he would ride that bicycle down that railroad Just imagine from The little town Not so played but the next little town Adrian I guess it was Up the road He would ride to Quincy to get there in time for the service on this bicycle. And then mother would take him home to dinner. He wasn't married. He had been married and he would have had a daughter my age. He was very fond of me. And I just adored him as a child. I can still remember him. His name was Herbert A. Corse. He was a big, lengthy man. But everybody just loved him. And she would bring him home to dinner. I mean we would. And then she would cook dinner. Cook chicken dinner for him. Well his wife died of childbirth. And so he married later. And we kept in touch with him. And I knew his second family after years later. But when he left Quincy. I was just a child. Just quite small. I don't know how old I was. Six or seven years old I suppose. And so he said Because of my two older sisters He said When you get ready to get married I'll come back and marry you. Oh I was going to assure him. He was talking to me Because I was his little favorite. So I always talked about When I was ten Mr. Corse was going to come back and marry him. And of course By the time I was that old I realized what he meant. He didn't mean he was going to marry me. He was going to perform the ceremony for all of us. But he never got to do that. But he was kind of a talented and interesting man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that The religion was particularly important to you in modern times?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh it was their life. It was why they Why they could do these things. Why they could take up homesteads. They had to have a They depended on it. They wanted to have faith. They knew that it would come out alright. And they did. Everything came out alright. Four of her children graduated from W .S. That was her aim in life. To have her children educated. The boys didn't graduate but the girls all did. I'm really curious about this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the women more involved in the church than the men?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Not in our family. My father was He was very musical. He was an Irish. His parents had come from Ireland. He had an Irish tenor voice. And he always led the singing in the Sunday school and church. And sang in the choir. Was the superintendent of the Sunday school. And my mother was so busy having children that He did more work I think that way than she did. She was back of him in all of it. Our family was my father.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: My family. I was his mother.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They had to do the work at home. But she saw to us that we were always clean and dressed. We always had to go to church. Made over and handed down clothes. You know everything was recycled in those days. But we didn't call it that. Nothing was ever wasted.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: At Sunday college everybody went to church. Yes we all went to church.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And we never missed. I got a little gold pin that I got for perfect attendance at Sunday school. I still got my little gold pin that I earned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you able to play any games on Sunday?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh well. Oh yes. We could play with our dolls but we couldn't. And we often had company and we could play. Go out and play. But it was limited somewhat. The day had to be. We had to know it was Sunday. And then sitting around in your Sunday clothes. That alone was a bit of a hardship you know. When you wanted to have on your play clothes. You kept them on if you had company. That made Sunday kind of a stick neck affair too I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what those early sermons were like?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh I don't suppose I ever heard the sermons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh the kids didn't?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I'll tell you what. Well. Children I don't think even yet listen very much to sermons.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Do they?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They learn in their Sunday school classes. I can remember things we learned in Sunday school classes. I had a lovely Sunday school teacher. And I can remember the Bible stories that we learned there. But I don't remember the sermons. I remember the singing. I know the songs that we sang. And I can remember mostly what the beautiful hats and the clothes that the women sitting in front of us wore. And you know people around. And the young couple that held hands under the song book. And that sort of thing. We were more interested in the children. We were more interested in the people in the church. Or the colored window. There was a colored memorial window in this church. This preacher I was speaking of had it put in in memory of his wife that died. And that church is still there in Quincy. The Presbyterians have built a big new church now that that little church is still there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a Presbyterian church there? Was it a community church?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well it was a Presbyterian church. But it seems to me that sometimes the Methodists also. That they kind of alternated. It seems to me there were years when they alternated. But it was built as a Presbyterian church. And when they first went there, there was no church. And they held services in a hall. They had a hall called the Dickerson Hall. And it was used for all sorts of community affairs. I think my sister in that story told about the big sagebrush that was used as the first community Christmas tree. Because they didn't have any evergreen trees. And later they shipped them in. But at that time they used this great big sagebrush. And stood up in the corner of the hall and they decorated it just like a Christmas tree. At home we had to burn sagebrush. But my mother, I don't remember that we ever took a sagebrush in for it. It probably was too dangerous because they burned easily. But we did burn sagebrush. And sometimes up in the drawers.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Do you know what a draw is?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We called it, a little ravine was called a draw. Where the spring runoff would wash the little leaves. They called them drawers in that country anyway. And the sagebrush that grew in these drawers up in the lower part of the hills grew very large. Sometimes as large as six or eight inches across the trunk. But around where we lived they were smaller. But those big ones made good fuel. And my dad would take the wagon and go up there in the fall and get a load of that for winter fuel. In later years they shipped in slab wood and we burned that. But the early days they burned sagebrush. So you see there was no expense. It was just labor. I would just mention it to Martin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It would take a lot more work wouldn't it to cut up sagebrush to burn sagebrush?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well you know it's soft. So it isn't hard to cut. And they just cut it with knives. I don't think they ever did. This big stuff they probably had to saw when they found those big ones. I don't remember that. One time Dad and the boys, the two older brothers went with him. And they took me. I was sort of a tomboy and I liked to go more. And there were two girls at home to help with the dishes and things. So I never really did much housework then. I still don't. I take it. But they took me with them. It was an all day trip with the wagon up into the hills to get it. And we took our lunch and I never cooked out. That was my first cookout. And Dad took potatoes and onions and he built a fire. And we roasted those. Oh I can still taste them. Oh were they ever good. I was just a little girl you know and I played. And there was big grass. Dad called it rye grass that grew around. Where they were cutting the sagebrush. And after the manner of children I built me a hut. You know children go through that stage of building all kinds of houses at different ages. And I built a hut out of this rye grass. And of course it probably was nothing but to me it was quite elegant. But one time they would pile the sagebrush up in the yard by the cellar. Safe distance from the house. One time it caught on fire from a spark and we lost it. And we had to go back and get a second supply. That was tragic of course. All that extra work. Cause you know it's very, burns real quick and hot.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: My grandparents and uncles and aunts, it's fine years we got from the well. They did serve them.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They served them.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: In fact they both well they didn't help.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They carried that land. The land at Quincy was real sandy and light. They were sandy in the house. All it needed was water.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: As they've grooved you know now. Every house had to be well plastered.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That's what they're true.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well our house wasn't plastered. And when there was a sandstorm coming, Mother would say children run and close the doors. But it was no use. The sand would come right in anyway. You couldn't close out. You couldn't keep it out. Those awful dust storms that they have in that country. They still have them. Of course now with the irrigation it isn't so bad. But south of Quincy they still have them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how were those slats tightened up?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh well board and batten that means boards that are put as close together as possible but there's still a crack. And then a board is put on the outside over that crack. And that's the kind of construction it was. Had a homestead house. That was homestead. Houses were built that way. And then sometimes they had. I don't think our house ever had anything but a tar paper roof. It had to be replaced often of course. I think it always had a tar paper roof. That was another fire hazard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you be warm in the winter?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well we had a cook stove. In this one room we had a cook stove and a heating stove. And of course at night the fires went out so then it was cold. And when you went to bed it wasn't extremely cold. Not like North Dakota colds that they've been used to. But it was cold at night in the winter. We had some snow usually. Always had some. They'd take a hot stone wrapped in an old flannel cloth to bed with you to heat your bed. Or a brick sometimes. We didn't have brick. We had stones or flat irons we'd take to bed. We had some hot water bottles but not enough to go around. So you'd take one of these. And you also took them when you went on sleigh rides too. You'd take those in the sleigh to keep your feet warm while you were on the sleigh ride. We went out in the morning to start fires. My dad and then later one of my brothers liked to get up. They'd get up and start the fires and put on the oatmeal. And start breakfast. We always had oatmeal at our house. Or for dinner. And it came in sacks. Everything came in cloth sacks in those days. Sugar and flour and cereals were all in cloth sacks. And I started to talk about recycling. That wasn't used as a word but everything was used over. And these sacks were of different quality. And the flour sack was a firmer material. And it was made into dish towels. And sometimes dyed and made into quilt tops. And everything was saved. You opened them so you didn't tear the sack. Then the sugar sacks were a finer cloth. And more if it was washed and starched a little it was almost like linen. And it was made into garments quite often too. And the underwear and nightgowns were made out of sacks. Of the finer quality softer sacks. And the small ones, the salt sacks were small. And they made good handkerchiefs and they were bleached. That was quite a chore in those days to get that printing out. They didn't always come out but if you were skillful with it you could get it out. And bleached, homemade bleach. Javel water and that type of bleach. And then my mother would hem them on the sewing machine and make the men's handkerchiefs out of the little salt sacks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did she grow up again?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, she came, her folks were New England people. And she had come, she was born in Wisconsin and got her education in Wisconsin. She was a normal school graduate and had been a teacher. And then she was, her folks moved to North Dakota and she was married there. And she taught in North Dakota before her marriage.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And then after that of course she didn't teach any more. Married women didn't teach in those days. And in these drawers, I was going to tell you about the food. We raised, they set out an orchard and it produced for quite a long time before the drought. The trees died by the time we left, there weren't too many left. We had cherry trees and peach trees and crab apple tree and some other apples. Quite a few cherry trees and we had some currants, some currant bushes. And then in the draw, these drawers where the spring runoff would hold the moisture for one thing and also the topsoil you see, so it was rich soil. My mother planted her early garden and we had radishes and leaf lettuce and those early things in that little garden in the draw. And then the other crops, corn and peas and squashes and those things were raised up in the orchard. But there was no irrigation, there was no water, so if it was a dry summer you didn't always get too much from that garden. But usually we had some garden and we always kept some pigs and a cow, milk cow. Made our own butter, if there was any excess, there wasn't much at our house but she could sell butter at the store to get a little grocery money. And we had a few chickens and of course, coyotes were plentiful and so chicken raising was hazardous. At one stage, after I had gone to school, my grandparents were having their golden wedding and it came after school started. So mother and the small children went to North Dakota to the golden wedding but those of us in school had to stay home and go to school. And I was the first one out, in the lower grades I would get out first, so my chore was to go home by myself, I always went home alone anyway. And I left the chickens out because if you left them out all day, dad was away working and these coyotes would come down and you wouldn't have any chickens. So they had to be kept cooped up but in those days chickens had to go out and rummage for food a bit and so they had to be let out. And so when I got home from school I had to let out the chickens and then I could play until the other children got home. And one night when I got home I was getting ready to fix, I fixed a teeter totter in front of the cellar. We had some apples in the cellar and I would get down and get me an apple to eat. And then I was alone but by using a big chunk of wood on one end of the teeter totter I could teeter totter pretty good, you know, get it to balance me and have fun with this apple. And I let the chickens out and I had one old pet rooster and here he came, got up on the teeter totter with me and he wanted some apple and I'd hold it behind me and there he'd go behind me to eat the apple and then I'd put it in front and there he'd be on the teeter totter. And I looked around and the next time to see where he was and here was a big coyote, just real close. Oh I was a bit scared but I knew the coyotes were cowards, my folks had taught us that to really not be too afraid of them. But that coyote wanted that rooster awful bad and then I got up and I yelled and he skedaddled and I was safe but I was a little scared after that to go home and let out the chickens because those coyotes were lurking all around. There were lots of them in that country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have the coyote chases?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well those were rabbits, well they did coyotes, they used to shoot all the coyotes, they could, they were so plentiful. They were so plentiful in the whole country. Quincy valley. But the rabbit, the jack rabbits were so plentiful and the coyotes fed on the jack rabbits you see and then when they killed off the coyotes, well then the jack rabbits practically took the country. And they were very hard on crops and we'd stack up the hay, in those days they used header boxes and you know there were no combines yet, they used a thrashing machine. And they were itinerant, they'd come around and thrash for you and go onto the next farm so you stacked up your wheat and then in the winter you would have a straw stack or a hay stack, you'd have some hay to feed the livestock. And these jack rabbits would come down and live off your hay stack and you just couldn't afford that. My father never owned a gun, he trapped the rabbits to try to keep them down but they got a lot of our grain that way you know because there were so many. And then in later years, I don't remember the rabbit drives but they had those rabbit drives that are historically interesting where they would build kind of a tight fence corral and run the rabbits in and then knock them down with clubs. It was very cruel but at the same time they had to get rid of them I guess. And now that country is all irrigated and they don't have that problem.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: After they got the syndrome, they had to clean up the tree.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That's right. In this gardening that we did, one time, my mother always had fun on holidays. We all had fun, no matter what the holiday was. We never spent any money but we had fun. May Day was one of the big days. We made May baskets and took our teachers May baskets. So my mother helped make this May basket for me to take my teacher when I was in the first or second grade. I don't know just which grade it was but I was real small. She took a shoe box, we never threw away boxes of any kind. They were real used for many things. She took a shoe box and she put some of these long white radishes that she'd raised. A bunch of those all scrubbed nice and clean. And some red radishes. The white ones were called white icicle radishes and they were real crisp and delicious. And a bunch of leaf lettuce and a little dish of homemade cottage cheese in this shoe box. She took a tissue paper to hold it firm and wrapped it up and put a big bunch of crab apple blossoms on it.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: The children would bring May baskets that had candy in them. I didn't like this idea at all but I never disobeyed my mother. So I took it to my teacher and it looked real pretty on the outside. It was wrapped with white tissue paper. That came out of the shoe box too. With this bunch of crab apple blossoms on top. She opened it and everybody was standing around looking and here inside was this stuff. This food. Well in those days there was no fresh stuff shipped into little towns. And if you didn't raise it you didn't have any. So my wise mother knew that would be the biggest treat that the teacher could have. Would be some fresh vegetables like that. And so I was the pet that day and it turned out alright but I didn't like the idea when I took it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the May Day celebration?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And that's one that I haven't heard much about. Well, I don't... May Day, at school we decorated the room for every occasion. You know the children made decorations for every holiday. I think they still do in school but it was big in those little schools. So for May Day, if we had flowers, we'd pick wildflowers and bring them and decorate the school room. And we would kind of have special programs. I don't remember anything special. We didn't... I don't remember winding the Maypole at that early age. I did later at Pullman when I was in... We used to have it out on the campus. The school children would wind the Maypole and I remember being in that one time when I was older. I don't remember it at Quincy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Halloween celebrated?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh yes, and it was a bad... it was in a bad way, you know. The boys went out and did all these tricks that you read about. And put wagons up on buildings and all those kind of things. But we lived so far from town that nothing bad ever happened at our house. And we didn't go trick and treating. I never did that as a child. We had neighbors, but there wasn't any way to go easily, you see, because we just had horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there the same business about goblins and witches and all that?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, yes, but it was fun. It was made fun. Stories, just stories at our house because we were by ourselves. We didn't have Halloween parties with anybody. Valentine's was a big day at our house. And it was all homemade. And one thing we used to make Valentine's was sample wallpaper books. You could get them free from the mail order houses. And they had these beautiful samples of... Sometimes they were about a foot by six inches, the size of the sheets. So it was big enough to make a nice Valentine. And then another thing we used for Valentine was the pink paper that came in the rubbers. When you bought rubbers, they came in a box and they were wrapped in pink tissue paper instead of white. And this was a great treasure, but it always smelled of the rubbers. That's the only thing we didn't like about it. It had an odor. But because it was pink, we thought it was pretty. And if you would take that and fold it, a piece of tissue paper, you can still do this, and fold it, and then cut it with scissors. Rather, not too fine, not clear through to the fold, but kind of fine. And then you pull that across the scissors, you can curl it. And then that would go around. We'd cut heart shapes, and then we would paste that around the Valentine's. And it made a very beautiful Valentine. My mother used to make us Valentine's too, as well as we made it for each other. And we made them for the children at school. And the one I treasured the most was one of these with this pink tissue paper. It was a cardboard. And she had saved a beautiful picture of a girl. My mother thought curls, and there wasn't a curl in our family, but curly hair. But she loved curly hair. And on this Valentine was a picture of a blonde girl with beautiful curls. And then all this curled pink paper around it. And on the back, my mother had beautiful handwriting. She had, they used to teach penmanship, you know, put great emphasis on penmanship. And her handwriting was beautiful. And on the back she had written my name, which was Martha Minnadel Lowry, and her beautiful handwriting. And then this little verse. Be good, sweet girl, but let who will be clever. I didn't know what that meant, but I knew it was something very lovely. I treasured that Valentine for years. In fact, we kept our Valentine's. We each had a brown manila envelope that something had come in that she had saved. And we saved these Valentine's. And we played with them for two weeks after Valentine's Day. And then they were put away. And the next year we got them out, and everybody had their own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the old ones were just as much fun as the new ones, you see?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That's the way she knew how to have fun without, to make use of what we did have. Everything was used. Everything was used, tin cans, why, when I look at the garbage, there was no garbage. The food garbage was fed to the pigs. Even the dishwater. All water was very precious at Quincy. And the dishwater was put in a special bucket, and that was called pig slob. Not a very elegant term, but that's what it was called. And it was fed to the pigs because there was a lot of nourishment in that dishwater, you know. And then the wash water and the bath water was put out to water the yellow rose and the matrimony vine. The only way they could be kept alive was from this water. And our water all had to be hauled. In the very early days it was hauled in barrels. In the very early days, when they first went there, it was hauled even on a stone boat. From a place called Willow Springs, which is about five miles away. But just think of that chore with no roads and a stone, you know what a stone boat is. There were no wheels on it. It was just a platform. Couldn't haul that much either. No, and the barrels of water, but later they hauled it in barrels in the wagon. And then later tanks. Somebody had a tank and our cistern was filled with a tank. But water was very precious. And all this paper doll work and Valentine's took paste. And we made our paste with flour, a little flour. And you would take a spoon because you couldn't waste water. I'm not sure I should tell this part is not very elegant. But we would, because water was so precious and there was a bucket of water with a dipper in the kitchen. And in trying to get some water into that paste you would waste water. So we couldn't do it very often, but we liked to have lots of paste. And sometimes we would spit in it. That's not very elegant, but that we actually did as children to get paste. But you would mix a little water with this flour and then you would have paste to make your paper dolls or your Valentine's or whatever. Scrapbooks, we made lots of scrapbooks too that way. Let me ask you another ... question.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the kids take care of bats?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, that, oh well, well, if they were small, but not older people never did. Not even though water was very scarce and very precious. And it was heated in two ways. It was heated in the wash boiler, on the stove, for the Saturday night baths were the most popular. And you sometimes had other baths, but always Saturday night baths. And you let down the oven door. There was no bathroom, no privacy. You let down the oven door for heat and the children were bathed earlier. And the water was heated in the reservoir. That was a part of the early cook stoves at the back. The small children were bathed together in the same water, but the older ones weren't. And then that water was thrown out on the rose bush and the garden maybe sometimes. And the chairs that we had were rather high backed. And if it was necessary to have a little privacy, those chairs with a blanket were arranged around this wash tub. It was the wash tub that the baths were taken in. We were clean. But it was somewhat of a sponge bath. No. Oh, it was a wash tub. It was a wash tub that anybody, any grown person could sit down in and curl up in. And, oh no. They weren't sponge baths. They were sponge baths, all right, taken in a basin. Martha tells her these stories about how I got this thing out.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We lived on a fruit ranch one time.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We had 70 acres and one broke. And we got so hard up up there on the branches when we were kids.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You young 'uns all come in here and get your ma's for you to make people think we got it coming in our house.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, gee, that's pretty good. That's his favorite story. That wasn't true, though. You just nagged your father telling that story. That's a good parra. We never went hungry on our farm. We didn't have lots of things, but we never went hungry. That being your orchard.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Where is it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Two crops, one down here at the finish. And a cold storage pack. We had that clean for one year. And come down here in spring and buy a gun.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We had to stop them in order to get the bluffs back. That's how we got out of the house. We had another Christmas custom that's kind of, that I don't think very many families would have thought of. This was my mother's invention. They'd find the biggest gunny sack, you know, a burlap sack. The biggest gunny sack, clean gunny sack. And in our, eventually, Dad built a stairway in this homestead house. And there was a loft. We never used it for anything but storage, but it was very useful as a place to put things, you know. And so, at Christmas time, this great big, about two months before Christmas, this great big gunny sack was hung about halfway up this stairway. Up where it was out of the way of living, but it was in sight. And then we made presents for each other. And as they were made, they were put into this sack. And that's the only time you were allowed to go up there near that sack, was when you had something to put in. But you could delay a little and sit on the step and kind of listen and maybe feel a little. No one was watching because the Christmas presents were accumulating in that sack and it was very tempting, but we never dared reach in and feel anything. But we could feel through the sack a little bit. We made all kinds of things for each other. And one of the things I remember making for my father, he shaved with a straight edge razor and a strop, you know. And there was no running water, so the soap, the shaving soap and the whiskers, as he would shave it off his face, had to be put someplace and he would use paper. So we would make him a shaving pad. We would take an old catalog. We always had mail order catalogs, these were book catalogs. And we would take the soft pages and cut them about six inches square. He liked that size. And we would make a pad of those papers. And then we would punch, with a darning needle, we'd punch a couple of holes in the top and make a pretty cover, maybe a piece of this, one of these wallpaper books. Or maybe we would draw a picture for the cover and then tie that with a piece of yarn and make him a shaving pad. And that was the kind of presence, homemade presence. We'd make my mother a needle book. The needle book was made with pieces of soft cloth that was blanket stitched around. That's a stitch that's one of the first stitches you learned as a little girl and with bright thread. And then you would put several of these pieces of cloth together with a cover and she would keep her needles in there. Or you would make a pin cushion and stuff it with something soft. And those are the kind of presents we made for grown -ups. And then the presents for the children were toys. And then of course, Santa Claus came too. That was separate. The Christmas sack was our presents to each other. That wasn't Santa Claus. But Santa Claus came Christmas Eve and we would hang up our stockings and what was in the stocking Santa Claus brought. About two weeks before Christmas, we would send our dolls to Santa Claus because Santa Claus knew how to make beautiful doll clothes. And we didn't get new dolls. We'd get new hair or a new head or new eyes, depending on what the doll might need. But we couldn't have a new doll at Christmas. I only had one or two new dolls my whole life. But I loved my old dolls, but they would be renovated, you see, by Santa Claus and my poor mother. I didn't know it then, but she would sit up after we'd gone to bed and fashioned these beautiful doll clothes. Christmas morning, we would get up. We could get up anytime we'd wake up. And there would be these. The stocking would be full. And then the hair would be this newly dressed doll sticking out of the top. That was the early day Christmas, but nothing was spent. An orange, every year we got an orange. That's the only orange I ever had was in the toe of my stocking. But they would buy an orange for each child. We'd get some hard candy and a Tarleton sack at the church program. And sometimes they'd buy some candy for Christmas. But most of the candy was homemade. We would decorate the house lavishly with paper chains made out of the wallpaper books and any colored paper you could find. Paper chains, that took more paste.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: But chains were fun to make. And we'd string popcorn and the house was decorated all over, hanging from the ceilings, great loops of all of this. And we'd sing all the Christmas carols. We were a great singing family. My father was, his father had come from Ireland and had been a singing school master. And Dad had done this too. And he taught us all to sing when we were very young. We'd sit around this big table, this white, cloth -covered table, before we had the organ. Later we got an organ, but he had a tuning fork. And he would strike the tuning fork and he would get the tone and everybody around the table would get the tone. Then we would sing. And he taught me to sing alto because I had a natural little voice. I was a big girl. And he was an Irish tenor. And he liked an alto sitting next to him, so he taught me to sing alto. I didn't know it was alto at that time, but he taught me how to sing the second part. And when I went to school, we sang. We sang, you know, American, all those songs.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Would you sing in school?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And the teacher, I was singing alto. It was the only thing I knew how to sing. The teacher stopped.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: She said, Martha, why are you singing like that?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And I said, that's the way I sing because I sit next to Papa and Papa taught me to sing like that. She knew Papa, so she said, okay, you can sing like that. But she didn't understand why a child my age was singing alto. But anyway, that's what I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the kinds of music that you sang at home? Was it a lot of religious music?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, we did gospel songs were popular, but all the old songs, old religious songs, we knew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But we always sang lots of fun songs, rounds like, oh, you know, what are some of the old rounds?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Row, Row, Row Your Boat. Yes, and in a big family that was great fun because there were a lot of us. And we sang songs like the Bullfrog on the Bank and the Bullfrog in the Pool. And Father could sing that real good where the high voices sing the Bullfrog on the Bank and then the low voices sing the Bullfrog in the Pool, you know, way down low. That was one of our fun songs. And then we sang, oh, there was one about, oh, my sister and I were talking about it this summer about McGinty.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: What was the old song about McGinty?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I can't think of that. Yankee Doodle was popular because my folks were New Englanders. They knew a lot of the New England songs. We knew all the old songs like Home Sweet Home and Coming Through the Rye and Annie Laurie. We had big song books that we sang from. And then later when I was still a child, the folks in North Dakota sent us an organ. And then that was a big day. And then we all had to learn to play the organ. My mother could play, and so she taught the girls. I never learned very much, but it was fun.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Did you hear the board speak to Warren?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, he didn't speak. But we had lots of music. Four older ones became a quartet, and they sang as a quartet in the church, the Four Older Men in the Family. But I was a child. Christmas, it sounds like, was really mostly family rather than a community. Well, it was both. It was both. There was a big celebration at the church where we spoke pieces and had special dresses. We had to dress up. They had big programs at the church, and they had a big Christmas tree, and they gave out sacks of candy and an orange sometimes in the sack. They were made out of colored tarlet and that, such stiff material. It's kind of like cheesecloth, but it was cheap and stiff, and it was pretty. And the women would get together and make these bags and fill them with hard candy and nuts. And everybody that came to the church program got one of those Christmas Eve. It was done on Christmas Eve. And some of the families took their gifts there. If they were going to give their children expensive dolls and nice things, they liked to show them off. And of course, that was hard on my mother because we didn't have those kind. And she would explain to us, this is how wise she was, that Santa Claus didn't bring our gifts to the church. He brought them to our house, and we were satisfied. We were never jealous. It was fun to see those beautiful dolls and trains and things that were displayed there. They'd bring them and put them under the tree, and then Santa Claus came and passed out the candy and gave these gifts to the children that had their names on them, but the poor little Lowry children never got any there. We didn't care because we knew ours were at home. It was all right. But it was a bad custom, really, for poor families. Some poor families didn't have it explained that way to them. We had lots of reading at home. My mother liked to read, and she read all the classics, you know, a lot of the good books to us. She liked Shakespeare, but she didn't read that to us as children. She read us poems and all the Mother Goose and the fairy stories and the East of Fables. We knew all of those, and books were real important. Books were treasured. She wanted us to learn about money, but it was real hard because there wasn't much. So one year, she assigned us jobs, and we would be paid. My job was to hold the weeds between two rows of trees in the orchard, and I was to get a dollar if I kept the weeds out all summer. Well, I wasn't a very good hoe -er, but I was learning that if you worked hard that you would be rewarded, you know, there was a reward. When I got my dollar, it was a silver dollar. And then was the question, what to do with it, and of course she guided me there, and made me want to buy books with it. I don't know. If she had let me alone, I probably would have bought candy or something else, as children do, but I was influenced to pick out some books because books, I thought books were great, and I bought two books for that dollar, and I still have one of them. I bought Beautiful Joe and the Heusier Schoolboy. I didn't care much about the Heusier Schoolboy after I bought it, but I like Beautiful Joe, and we all cried. We used to all cry when we read these sad stories about, you know, black beauty and Beautiful Joe and these mistreated animals that made you cry. My mother cried, so I cried. Beautiful Joe. Later I rebounded that little book, but that was bought with my first dollar. Beautiful Joe is a dog. A dog that was mistreated, you know, and it was a sad story. Children liked those sad stories. We all cried. We cried a lot when Mother read to us because she cried over poems, all the sad poems, you know.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: But that's still a good educational way of getting children interested in books, isn't it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I mean, children need to be interested in books, and they have to start young.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seems like crying was a lot more common in those days, wasn't it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, my mother was very emotional, and some people weren't, some weren't, but, you know, I think maybe they did cry a lot. Not always sad. I was happy crying,

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: too.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You're right. I'm on it.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Nowadays, you take it so much more as weakness, you know, to cry than things. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. I guess so.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Although, I guess that it's probably always been regarded that way for men more. Eye specialists say it's good for your eyes. Yeah, tears are good for your eyes. Necessary.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, the hair won't be redone.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Let's see, what else did we recycle?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I think I've told you most everything I thought of. Oh, the tin cans. We used every tin can and every bottle, except poisons. And they were thrown, you know where. They were thrown out in the outhouse because they couldn't be washed up like carbolic acid. But all the other containers were reused in various ways for things. And some of the bottles were used to give the horses their medicine, you know, and some of the bottles were used to store things in that you had. Mother made eye water and it was put in a bottle. I can remember a lot of medicines were homemade and they had to have a bottle. And cans were, baking powder cans had a tight lid. And the baking powder, the cover of the baking powder can was Mother's cookie cutter and biscuit cutter. That's all she ever had was a lid. And the cans were used to store things in because there were no tight cupboards or places to store things. You had to have tight containers for everything. Yes. Yes, you had varmints of different kinds, insects and mice and rats. And then the only can I can think of that couldn't be used that way was the milk can. And condensed milk was used by, when the cow went dry you had to have some condensed milk, you know. And sometimes it was used for baby formulas. There weren't any actual baby formulas. But if a mother wasn't able to nurse the baby, then condensed milk. And I don't mean canned milk like we have it, although that was available too. But condensed milk which was sweeter was used and babies got real fat on it. It wasn't good but that's what they used. They used it just straight. I mean they just used it the way it was. They didn't mix it with other foods. And so there was the milk cans that we had then. And they were even recycled. And if you jammed them just right you could make them stick to your heel. And they were great fun to run around on, sort of like a little stilt. And we played games and then they were used as a game like hockey with a stick. And you bat the can, you know, hit the can.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Wasn't that what it was called?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Shinny on your own side. And now that's it. I don't think we ever called it that though, but we used to play that. And that's remarkable, you know, that people try to go to these old homesteads and dig up the dumps. Well there weren't any. They didn't throw anything away except broken things. Broken glass and broken bottles and broken dishes were thrown away but not anything else. They started in alright. There was sickness and a bad day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you deal with it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: There were just a few home remedies that were drastic. There was always castor oil. And if you got just, you didn't try to show you had a little stomach ache. Because if you got it, you got a dose of castor oil. And it was drastic. And that was one of the common ones and everybody used it. And then for cuts and small injuries of that kind, there was turpentine was used in our family. A great deal of that would heal up anything. Nowadays it won't, but it did then. And we had a few rotten things. We had Listerine when I was quite young. I remember Listerine, which was considered an antiseptic of a sort. And then there was carbolic acid for real severe injuries. But we were a real lucky family. We never had any real serious illness. That was dangerous to you. And then if you got a cold, the colds were common. It wasn't called flu. The colds were common and my mother had a drastic treatment for that. It was kind of like an Indian sweat bath in a way. She would take the big rocking chair and put a big quilt in it and then the blankets, the wash blankets, you know. And put you in your nightgown in that chair and take a five gallon oil can. We always had those. They were salvaged too. The ones at the care scene came, the top was cut out carefully so they made a container. And you put your feet in that with hot water and mustard. And you soaked your feet in that and you sat in this chair all wrapped up in this blanket and drank hot lemonade and then you would sweat. And that took everything out of you that you didn't have as well as what you did have. And you never had a cold afterwards. And then as soon as you thought you'd been there long enough, why, and you'd sweat enough, why she would get the bathtub out and have the room good and hot and give you a bath and put you in bed until you cooled off. Because, you know, it's dangerous to get chilled. But that was a standard remedy. And oh, a mustard plaster on your chest and one on your back. And they were hot and they blistered you. And they were homemade, made out of the same mustard that you ate on your hand. You made that by mixing it up and sometimes she put a little flour in that reduced the heat so it wasn't quite so bad. But she'd take a piece of an old sheet and stir up this mustard water and lather it out on the sheet, fold it up and put that on you. Put a flannel over it. That would sure cure a cold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it work good?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We never had any. We were always healthy so I guess it worked. We never died either. That was a miracle. Sure it worked. She was real careful of us. But we never had anything but children's diseases. We got that kind of treatment quite often if she thought we had the sniffles. We had to wear flannels. We had to wear our rubbers and it didn't rain. It never rained there. We had to have rubbers.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You know what we used to do with them?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I think my sister told that story. We used to hide them under the sage bush. No, that was awful. Guilt was worse than being punished, you know. And I was so scared she'd find out. I'd hide mine under the last fence before I'd go to school. I'd hide my rubbers. There wasn't any rain. There was no need of wearing them but we had to wear them. And the kids would make fun of you if you wore your rubbers to school in dry weather, you know. So then I'd worry all day for fear of somebody to steal them and they wouldn't be there. We were home again. She was a stern mother but she was a wonderful mother. She taught us the things that were good. Honesty and good language. She couldn't stand slang. There was no swearing in our family. But slang even. One time I learned the word Dickens from one of my little girlfriends. And we were out hanging up clothes. I was holding the clothespin bag for her and I dropped the clothespin and I said to Dickens, Boy, she grabbed me just like that and in the house I went and had my mouth washed out with tar soap. And I never said that and I felt guilty. I was grown before I ever said that again. It wasn't swearing exactly, you know, but it was slang and she wouldn't put up with it.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No wonder she's such an angel.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. Well afterwards, and I grew up, it was fun to say that because I'd been punished and I realized it wasn't really bad. It was just she was a real strict, you know, she liked the English language the way it ought to be said and she taught us that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't mean that your kids did not do things that you knew were wrong? That you go ahead and do those things like the rubbers anyway?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, that we did, but that, you see, I suffered. I suffered from it because I was guilty all, I felt guilty all day, I was scared all day. So it wasn't good for us. We didn't defy her very often but it was easier to be guilty than to face the kids at school. Because, you know, kids are rough on each other. Tell that story about the kids at school that spoke different languages. Oh yes, we had, well we were, my folks were English I guess you would say. I mean they'd come from, dad had come from Michigan. He was Irish but they spoke what we called English and then there was one family of Bohemians and then there were a lot of these German families had moved into Quincy that farmed. And they spoke German and the parents often couldn't speak English and the children didn't learn English until they went to school so that there was a lot of Germans spoken on the playground, you know. They had to speak English in school, they had to learn but they would speak German and then this one family that were Bohemian, they spoke Bohemian I guess it would be.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And then they would say, now what do you speak?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well I didn't speak anything.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And I went home and I said to my mother, what's wrong with us, these other children, they speak German and they speak Bohemian and we don't know anything, what are we?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: My mother said, it was quite incensed to think that I'd been attacked like that and she said, yes you are. She said, you're Irish. My grandparents had come from Ireland, my father's parents. She said, you're Irish and she was proud of the Irish blood in our family and next time that they, but I said I don't. Well you know, children, children have to be, they want to be all alike.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: If one can do something then you know, why can't you do it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You know, so she said, I said that I can't speak Irish. She said, well not tomorrow and she went over to the corner of the kitchen where she cooked and got into the potato sack and took out a little potato and scrubbed it up and she said, now tomorrow you put this in your pocket. And when you go to school, when they say that to you, because they would again, she said, you say I'm Irish and they'll say, alright, talk some and then you say, I'm Irish and if you don't believe it I can show it to you or have it in my pocket. And then you pull out this Irish potato. And sure enough they asked me the next day and I showed them the potato and that cooked and that cooked with nagging. They didn't bother me anymore. So we were proud of being Irish. My dad could sing songs and things to us and he played the flute and he played the juice harp and he could stand on his head and he entertained us that way. He was a real performer. He seemed to be on the dead side. Yes, dad could stand on his head. That was one of his accomplishments until he was quite old. He lived to be in his 80s. I don't remember when he quit doing it but scared mother to death. None of us could do it but he always could stand on his head. He'd go down and put out his hands and bring up his feet. He could stand perfectly straight up on his head. We thought that was great. He was quite an accomplishment. He wasn't a heavy man. He was a big man, tall man but he was slim. He danced. He danced the jig. But dancing wasn't allowed in our church or anything. My mother didn't believe in dancing. She let dad dance the jig but he never got to teach it to us. I wish he had but he didn't because she didn't believe in dancing. But I guess maybe he did because he could share dancing Irish jig. You said it was one way or the other with families as far as religion goes. Either they were strict or they weren't strict at all. Oh yes. Well these little towns all had saloons. There were people that didn't go to church and just lived a different life than we did. There was a saloon. I didn't know what... it wasn't discussed much in our family. My mother was such a temperance person. Coming home from school there were boardwalks in this little town and I had to go... this walk came up past the hotel and there was a saloon, apparently a saloon in that hotel. I didn't know it at the time but that's what it was. But this odor, I would know this odor and later then I found that was the odor of whiskey but at the time I didn't know that's what it was. As I would come up the street I could smell this. I always wondered what it was but I never knew as a child if that's what it was. Then there were lots of bottles. In fact my sister told about my mother's rolling pin being a beer bottle, brown beer bottle. There were lots of liquor bottles around. Sometimes they would use them for things but there was never any liquor in our house. No smoking either. No dancing, no card playing. There was no liquor in our house.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They all drank.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well my brothers did too when they got older. That didn't necessarily mean that they grew up that way but that's the way they were brought up. That's the way it was. It wasn't necessarily a good way but that's the way it was. But there were probably some people that would also go and still go to church even though they might dance. Oh yes, that's right. The Dickerson Hall that I mentioned was where they had dances but I didn't know much about them because we didn't go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she at all active as a teetotaler? Was there any opportunity?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I don't think so. I don't think so at that time. No. No women's organization or anything. Her folks had been that way. It was the way she'd been brought up. Lots of families were like that.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Can you tell us a little bit about the cards on the train?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, the playing cards. I'd never seen playing cards. We had flinch and muggin. We played muggin with flinch cards. We played card games. We played Old Maid and Authors. It was great. That's the way we used all the cards. But not playing cards because playing cards were associated with the devil in the religious training of those early days. And that's the way gambling. You couldn't play cards and not become a gambler. That was the supposition. So I'd never seen playing cards. I'd just seen these flinch cards which were bright colored. They were grey, green and white or brown and white or blue, maybe sometimes sort of rosy and white. They were pretty designs on the cards that we had. And of course Old Maid cards had pictures on them and Authors cards had pictures. I was four years old and my mother went back to North Dakota to see her folks. I went with her because a child that age could travel without any fare. The other children were in school and I was the youngest. So I went with my mother. On the train in those days they had a table. They would bring you a table that was put up between the seats. The seats would face plush seats that faced each other. There was a table between. You could eat your lunch. You carried your lunch in a shoebox. You could eat your lunch on that table or you could play games or whatever you wished. So the people in the seats behind us were playing cards. I stood up in the seat and looked over and the whole thing was very exciting to me. I remember it distinctly. I don't remember much when I was four but this I remember because it was all different. The train and the plush seats, red plush seats. Standing up and we were going to see Grandpa and Grandma besides which was great excitement. I stood up and these people were playing with these beautiful cards. I was so excited. I said, Mother look. Come and look. She looked and yanked me down. Boy. No, you must never look at those. You must never play with those. I didn't know why. I didn't understand why. I just knew they were beautiful and those people were having fun. It was just too bad it couldn't have been explained better. I didn't learn to play cards until I was through college and out in the world. My mother was gone by then but she probably would have changed by then too. I became a good card player. You probably danced too. Yes, I learned to dance in college. I had to take lessons though because I was so old at that time that everybody else knew how they learned along the way. People were supposed to learn things but I hadn't so I had to go to Saturday afternoon on the campus. They had a class because there were lots of college kids brought up just like I had been that didn't know how to dance. We learned to dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the idea of socializing? The form of socializing if you respected these dancing and cards and that sort of thing and putting aside services?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You played games. You had lots of games. All kinds of indoor games.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were adults now?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh yes. You had socials. Then you had games like Peacehead Checkers and those games that you played between two or three or four people. Dominoes were a great favorite. Families would go visiting and play games at each other's houses or you would have a social. I don't remember going to many of those in the town. The traveling was a little too difficult but town families did get together and have them. You played games like Farmer in the Dell and Skip to My Lou. We played those even in my college days at church socials. We played Spin the Platter and Winkum and Post Office. My mother didn't approve of Post Office. But they did play. That was a kissing game. But Winkum was okay. She let us play Winkum but not Post Office. Off the list.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was the Miller Boy and those party songs?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Were they part of the... Anything Skip to My Lou. You had a partner who was really dancing. It was like folk dancing. You had a partner, you held hands and you skipped around the room. It was a sort of folk dancing. Those were allowed but not social dancing. It was mostly that dancing and card playing went along with saloons and the rough life that they were trying to protect us from. That was the reason. It wasn't the thing itself. There wasn't anything wrong with it. But it led to it. Where it was done. There was drinking at public dances. So you would be led into this temptation. That's why it was prohibited. We didn't do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But like in Skip to My Lou, would there be any musical accompaniment?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh yes. Somebody played the piano. We played a lot of musical games. Farmer in the Dell. That was fun because the farmer chose his wife. If you got to be his wife, that was a big night. If it was your favorite boy, those were kid games but adults played them too. The whole family would play together. And then Spin the Platter, you were a circle and somebody was it. And you spun the platter and if you could grab it before it stopped then you were it. They had a lot of old games that were fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As far as socializing with other families, would it mostly be your family visiting another family's house?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, we did that a lot. That was mostly in the daytime because of travel. And going to Sunday dinner. They were too busy during the week to do too much. On holidays we'd get together. And the 4th of July was always celebrated with a big celebration. Several families getting together.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And the church either went home with somebody or they... Yes.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And one home that we used to go home to dinner was more ornate and they had more money than we did. In fact she had Victorian furniture that she kept stored and covered in one bedroom because of the dust. And she didn't use it as she would have. And on that, that was a papered house and it had big pink roses in the wallpaper and I thought that was just the end. That beautiful pink rose patterned wallpaper. Because we didn't have any wallpaper in our walls. I thought to have a house like that must be just very wealthy. And she had an ostrich and she would give us feathers from the ostrich. They were great treasures. And one time for Easter she took an ostrich, not an ostrich, I'm sorry, I meant a peacock. She would give us the peacock feathers. They were so beautiful. And a peacock egg is quite large, not as large as the ostrich egg. She didn't have an ostrich, she had a peacock. And one Easter she hard boiled a peacock egg and coloured it and gave it to me for a present. And I kept it. My mother let me keep it. I kept it upstairs in the loft. Of course I suppose it finally dried up instead of... it rotted probably first and then dried up. It was one of my treasures I kept for a long time.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: To me it was huge but I don't think peacock egg is actually so much bigger than a duck egg, is it?

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Probably twice as big as a chicken's egg. Yes, it was a big egg compared to the chicken eggs that we'd coloured for Easter.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, you get an idea how we lived, didn't we? Haven't you?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, there was another little fun thing that we got out of them. Anything you got free, you know, was made fun. We used baking soda, lots of baking soda in cooking and then it was also a home remedy for upset stomachs and bee stings and all kinds of things. It still is a good remedy. But in the baking soda package there was a little card of little birds and it was a little premium or a little gift with the package. And then we collected those and when we bought a new package of baking soda which cost a nickel, this little card was attached to it and it was someone's turn to have it at that time. You know, you couldn't always get the card yourself but you would save those and then you would have a collection. They were beautiful pictures of birds and now they would be an antique collection if anyone has any of those. You keep your eye out because you might find them.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: A dime?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I thought it was a nickel.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Are you sure?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, you're the merchant so you'd know but anyway, baking soda went a long ways. We had baking powder biscuits and it didn't take much to make a pan of biscuits. These men that went out to work on the road did their own cooking and they made soda biscuits and they learned to make awful good ones and so when they came home then our treat was for the men, my brothers and dad, to make the biscuits for supper. They were crisp, had crust. Soda biscuit had a nice crust to it. But you always had biscuits. My mother didn't think so much baking powder bread was good for you. She always had biscuits but dad had that. Now you see that was the difference between New England and Southern. His mother was a Southern cook and she always made biscuits. My mother was New England and they were raised on yeast bread and she didn't think so much baking powder bread was good for you. So we just had it sort of like emergency bread and you ran out of yeast bread and you had baking powder bread.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How hard do you feel that your parents had to work there?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh they worked hard. We got up early and there was stock to take care of and there was the farming to do and everything had to be made, clothes had to be made, butchering, the butchering you know was hard work and everything was made and there was no refrigeration so butchering was a problem to keep the meat. In the winter there was no problem. I wonder what the chores had to be done. The children helped, everybody worked. They had to milk twice a day. Yeah you milked the cow twice a day and then you put it in pans and you put it in the cellar and the cream rose and you skimmed off the cream and then the milk that was left was still real rich but the cream was made into butter. You churned the butter in a dash or churn. Children had to help do that with a dash or churn. It took a long time to get the butter to come. Mother made the butter. You should tell the verse, the verse by your churn. Well to make things easier we were taught to sing. We sang a lot when we did chores like that. We sang this churn butter churn. I forgot the rest of it. Peter's waiting at the gate waiting for a buttered cake and we sang this little verse and that made the time go faster. Then we finally had a washing machine. First of course it was just a washboard but then we got a washing machine that had a wheel that had to be turned. It didn't get the clothes very clean but the boys and I had to turn it sometimes. We would sing or we would read stories while we turned this and of course if the story got too exciting you'd forget the turn. It wasn't a very good system but the children had to help with all these chores. We gathered the eggs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that you and your brothers and sisters were really close to each other?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh yes we were a close family I should say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not very much conflict among you?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh no there wasn't any. No they were real close. There was no jealousy or anything. I don't know what you think.
